biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron (Fe3+) stimulates C. difficile growth in a dose-dependent manner
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure1a: Fe3+ at10 µM increased CFU3.5-fold, peaking at100 µM with13.8-fold increase in strain9689. Co-treatment with DFO reduced CFU.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron upregulates expression of toxin genes tcdA and tcdB
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure1b:50 µM Fe3+ increased tcdA mRNA2.2-fold and tcdB mRNA3.3-fold relative to control (p <0.05).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron increases production of toxin A protein
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure1c: Western blot showed dose-dependent increase in toxin A:2.6-,3.8-,5.5-, and8.6-fold at10,20,50,100 µM Fe3+ respectively.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Normalized to CFU, iron does not increase toxin A per cell, suggesting increased growth is the primary driver
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure1d: After normalizing toxin A band density to CFU, no significant change was observed vs control.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron increases minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of metronidazole in susceptible strains
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure2a: In strain9689,50 µM Fe3+ increased MTZ MIC from0.17 µg/mL to1.38 µg/mL at48 h. No effect on vancomycin MIC (Figure2b).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron chelator deferoxamine reverses iron-induced growth, toxin production, and metronidazole resistance
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure3b: DFO (50 µM) reduced CFU to45.8% of control. Figure3c: DFO co-treatment reduced toxin A from5.2-fold to2.1-fold. Figure3d: DFO restored MTZ MIC to baseline.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron may increase MIC of fidaxomicin but not vancomycin
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Figure2c: In strain9689,50 µM Fe3+ increased fidaxomicin MIC6.7-fold at24 h, but remained <1 µg/mL.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Iron overload leads to increased susceptibility of C. difficile to metronidazole tolerance via iron metabolism
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Discussion cites earlier proteomic analysis linking iron metabolism to metronidazole resistance; Boekhoud et al. found haem (iron-containing) increased MTZ MIC8-25 fold.

key_findings: Excess iron (Fe3+) stimulates C. difficile growth and toxin production in a dose-dependent manner in vitro.
Iron exposure increases expression of tcdA and tcdB mRNA and increases toxin A protein levels.
Iron increases the MIC of metronidazole and fidaxomicin but does not affect vancomycin susceptibility.
Iron chelator deferoxamine effectively reduces iron-induced growth, toxin production, and restores metronidazole susceptibility.
The hypervirulent strain R20291 is intrinsically metronidazole-resistant (MIC >64 µg/mL) independent of iron supplementation.
The increase in toxin A with iron is partially attributable to increased bacterial cell density rather than increased per-cell toxin production.
Iron may contribute to metronidazole treatment failure in iron-overloaded patients, supporting vancomycin as first-line therapy.
relevance_to_research_topic: This paper provides in vitro evidence that excess iron directly promotes C. difficile growth and toxin production, and reduces susceptibility to metronidazole and fidaxomicin. These findings are highly relevant to the research topic investigating the effects of a high-iron diet on CDI in mice, as they suggest dietary iron overload could exacerbate infection by enhancing pathogen fitness and altering antibiotic responses. The paper also highlights the potential role of iron chelation as a therapeutic strategy, which aligns with studying 'diet-gut microbiota-host' interactions in CDI susceptibility.
